author: @MrDkgio
text:
You could clip the spare wires, use them as a “heater coil”(with the length of the ground and +ve wires) 

With using thin wires @ 5v do not use less than 1.35 metres length, experiment with length to get the desired temperature. Start long and shorten until desired temperature reached 👍

Heated gloves, mittens, insoles, jackets, Ebike battery warmer for cold weather.

A single 1850 2600mah battery lasts about 2 hrs

An Ankar powerbank 20100 mah lasts significantly longer 7.8 hrs

author: @G1gop
text:
A couple of minor points. A lot of 'fake' battery banks won't deliver 2.5A and will go into shutdown. also (teaching my grand mother to suck eggs here lol) DON'T plug it into the USB port on your computer! As you know (  :)  )  USB 2 is only specc'd to 500ma and even a lot of so called USB 3 ports can't provide. also that has to come from you power supply so a laptop could get hot!. Good idea and simple though. done similar in the past with power resistors.
As an aside I found out our cooker hood carbon filter (usually easy to get locally) is carbon felt! prob not a cheap as buying in a roll size for size but might for some be easier to get?

author: @NdxtremePro
text:
This is cool. You can buy clothes that have essentially this inside them on Amazon with the batteries. Socks are a great choice.
Also, if you were wanting to use a bit more power, you could get a USB-C kit I linked to in a previous video of yours that will let you switch between 6,9,12,15, and 20 volts at 5 amps. This would give you more options in sizing the heater, as well as the ability to choose different temperatures.
These are only a few bucks and are a great way to transform your older electronics to USB-C power.

replies.text: Technically anything above absolute zero radiates energy, but 10 watts is not very much, and if it only gets to 30 C you're not going to feel it more than an inch or two away.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Just a note. Over the years I have found that some USB cables use very thin copper or even aluminium which results in a high resistance in the cables. This is due as those cables probably came with a small mp3 player which only needed a low charge current hence higher resistance cables did not make any difference. I've actually seen cables with 1 ohm in the positive and 1 ohm in the negative wire.  So there is 2 ohms in the cable already.. Rather nasty so best watch out.

author: @DavidMartin-jr8nd
text: I love this and might even make one. Just so people know some more modern USB plugs require a "handshake" before they provide power. I dont think this will work with those. I don't know all the details, but i think its mostly USB C that requires a handshake. So if you do as seen here and use the big end ( USB A) and snip the little end you are probably ok.

replies.author: @joshpoole6056
replies.text: Is that why certain phones won't charge unless you use the specific charger that came with it?

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: USB power defaults to 5V, that's always there. If you want more, you have to do the handshake business. Higher power modes also depend on a certain arrangement of resistors in the cable, and some companies cheap out on those components.

replies.author: @DavidMartin-jr8nd
replies.text: Thanks for the clarification DFP!

author: @MiniatureChickenChannel
text: LOVE this channel!  ♨♨.........now i'm curious....if you make your carbon fiber stip really short....can you heat put it under your coffee cup and use as a "hot plate" LOL.....or will it pull too much current from the battery pack and "smoke" it?  I assume there's a protection circuit there that limits how many amps?.......on the better quality packs that is......I'm sure you can burn you house down with some cheaper units lol.🔥🔥🚒🚒

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: Any battery or power supply will have a rated max output in terms of watts or amps. Just make sure your load doesn't pull more than that. If it does, you might be able to double up the supply by connecting them in parallel. Be careful doing that with lithium batteries though. If one is charged more than the other, they will try to equalize and draw much higher current. Also make sure you're using thick enough wires to handle the current. The total resistance of the circuit shouldn't be significantly higher than the resistance of the heating element itself. A coffee cup warmer does sound like a fine idea though.

replies.author: @MiniatureChickenChannel
replies.text: @DFPercush Roger that...thank you for the tips!  I was just reading about individual cells that will balance themselves if connected with a dis-similar State-of-charge this morning!
